title: Generating collective spin cat states via photon-number measurements near the Dicke critical point
authors: Taiga Nakamoto, Shohei Imai, Kazuaki Takasan
abstract: We propose a method for generating collective spin cat states in a cavity-coupled atomic ensemble by exploiting strong light-matter entanglement and anti-squeezing associated with the superradiant phase transition. We numerically and analytically demonstrate that the cat states can be heralded by photon-number measurement on the ground state of the Dicke model. The near-critical regime enhances both the cat-state size and the probability of obtaining larger photon-number outcomes, and outcomes with larger photon numbers yield even larger cat states. We also show that a thermodynamic-limit analysis clarifies the generation mechanism and connects it to a natural light-matter analogue of generalized photon subtraction for optical cat-state generation. These results suggest that exploiting criticality in strongly coupled light-matter systems could open new directions for matter-based many-body quantum technologies.
summary: This theoretical work proposes a method to generate large, high-fidelity collective spin cat states in an atomic ensemble by leveraging the physics near the superradiant phase transition. The key technique involves performing a photon-number measurement on the cavity field, which acts as a quantum filter to herald the desired non-classical atomic state. This demonstrates a powerful synergy between cavity QED criticality and quantum state engineering.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of generating high-fidelity collective spin cat states (SCSs) within a cavity-coupled atomic ensemble.
main_result: They demonstrate that performing a photon-number measurement on the cavity field near the superradiant critical point heralds SCSs with enhanced size and fidelity.
method: The protocol relies on exploiting strong light-matter entanglement and anti-squeezing inherent to the critical regime, mathematically linking the process to generalized photon subtraction.
model_or_system: The system is modeled by the Dicke model, describing N two-level atoms coupled to a single cavity mode. The analysis utilizes the Hamiltonian and examines the system's behavior near the superradiant phase transition.
key_observables: SCS size ($\ell_{	ext{opt}}$), Fidelity ($F(	heta)$), Wigner function negativity, and the probability distribution of measured photons ($P(n)$).
important_parameters: The critical coupling strength ($g_c$), the atom number ($N$), and the photon number ($n$) measured.
main_assumptions: The analysis often focuses on the resonant case ($\omega_{	ext{cav}} = \omega_{	ext{atom}}$) but confirms the mechanism's applicability off-resonance. It assumes the ground state of the equilibrium Dicke model.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the protocol schematic (Fig. 1a), the Wigner function showing SCS non-classicality (Fig. 1b), and quantitative dependencies of $\ell_{	ext{opt}}$ and $P(n)$ on the coupling strength $g$ and the photon number $n$ (Figs. 2 and 6).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Dicke model and the concept of SCSs. It then details the measurement-based protocol, showing how photon detection projects the atomic state into a heralded SCS. Finally, it analyzes the scaling laws and the physical mechanism connecting this to generalized photon subtraction.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, measurement-based pathway to generate complex, non-classical many-body states (SCSs) using controllable quantum optical phenomena (criticality), which is highly relevant for quantum simulation and quantum memory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic picture of the protocol for generating SCSs. We first prepare an atomic ensemble that is strongly coupled to the cavity field near the critical point of the super- radiant phase transition. We then perform a photon-number measurement on the cavity field; conditioning on a photon- number outcome heralds the preparation of an SCS in the atomic ensemble. (b) Schematic illustration of the Wigner function of the SCSs. We focus on the SCS, which is a super- position of two coherent spin states pointing in the directions (+θ, 0) and (−θ, 0). We characterize the size of the SCS as an arc length Jθ normalized by the quantum fluctuation p

FIG. 2. The Wigner function W(θ, ϕ) around θ = 0 of (a) the unheralded state ρ = Trphoton[|G⟩⟨G|] and (b)-(g) the n-photon heralded atomic state |ψn⟩for the outcomes n = 1, . . . , 6. The Wigner function of ρ is Gaussian-like, while that of |ψn⟩has negative values and interference patterns. The interference patterns in the Wigner function indicate the production of SCSs. Heralding converts the Gaussian-like state into a non-Gaussian state with parity-dependent interference fringes. (h) The size of the SCS lopt as a function of n. When n is even (odd), the parity of the SCS is even (odd). lopt increases as the measured photon number n increases. The fidelities F(θopt) between the ideal SCS...

FIG. 3. (a) The size of the SCS lopt for n = 1, . . . , 6 as a function of the coupling strength g. As g approaches the critical point gc, lopt increases. Especially, lopt is significantly enhanced near the critical point g = gc. The fidelities F(θopt) are more than 0.988 for all the data points. (b) The measure- ment probability of n photons P(n) as a function of g. As g approaches gc, P(n) exponentially increases. Here, we set N = 200.

FIG. 5. The interpretation of the SCS generation protocol from the perspective of the generalized photon subtraction protocol in quantum optics [31]. Near the critical point of the superradiant phase transition, the ground state prepares the beam-split squeezed states. One mode corresponds to the cavity field, and the other mode corresponds to the collective spin excitation of the atomic ensemble. The photon-number measurement on the cavity field can conditionally prepare a SCS similar to the quantum optical case.

FIG. 4. (a) The size of the SCS lopt for n = 1, . . . , 6 as a function of the atom number N. lopt increases as N increases. We also plot the saturation value in the thermodynamic limit as dashed lines (Eq. (23)). lopt approaches the saturation value as N increases. The fidelities F(θopt) are more than 0.988 for all the data points. (b) The measurement probability of n photons P(n) as a function of N. P(n) increases as N increases for small N, while P(n) starts to decrease for large N. Here, we set g = gc.

Spintronics.note: The paper explicitly models the system using the Dicke model (a quantum-optical description of interacting spins) and discusses cavity QED physics near a phase transition, directly overlapping with the theme of cavity QED/Dicke physics in hybrid spin-photon systems.
